back_translation: Now Ben-hadad king of Aram assembled his entire army, and thirty-two kings were with him, along with horses and chariots. He marched up, besieged Samaria, and waged war against it.
He sent messengers into the city to Ahab king of Israel,
saying, “This is what Ben-hadad says: ‘Your silver and gold are mine, and your best wives and children are mine.’”
And the king of Israel answered, “Just as you say, my lord the king: I and all that I have are yours.”
The messengers came again and said, “This is what Ben-hadad says: ‘I sent to demand your silver, your gold, your wives, and your children.
But about this time tomorrow I will send my servants to search your palace and the houses of your servants, and they will seize and carry off everything you treasure.’”

Then the king of Israel summoned all the elders of the land and said, “See now how this man is looking for trouble; for he sent to me for my wives, my children, my silver, and my gold, and I did not refuse him.”
And all the elders and all the people said to him, “Do not listen to him or consent.”

So he said to Ben-hadad’s messengers, “Tell my lord the king, ‘All that you demanded of your servant the first time I will do, but this thing I cannot do.’” And the messengers departed and brought back the reply.
Then Ben-hadad sent to him, saying, “May the gods deal with me, and ever so severely, if enough dust of Samaria remains for each of the people who follow me to have a handful.”
And the king of Israel answered, “Tell him: ‘Let not the one putting on his armor boast like the one taking it off.’”
When Ben-hadad heard this message as he and the kings were drinking in their tents, he said to his servants, “Take your positions.” So they took their positions against the city.

Meanwhile a prophet approached Ahab king of Israel and said, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Do you see all this great army? Behold, I will deliver it into your hand this day, and you will know that I am the LORD.’”
“By whom?” Ahab asked. The prophet replied, “This is what the LORD says: ‘By the young officers of the district governors.’” “Who will begin the battle?” Ahab asked. “You will,” he answered.
So Ahab mustered the young officers of the district governors, and there were 232. After them he mustered all the rest of the Israelite troops, 7,000 in all.
They marched out at noon while Ben-hadad and the thirty-two allied kings were getting drunk in their tents.
The young officers of the district governors marched out first. Now Ben-hadad had sent scouts, who reported to him, “Men are marching out of Samaria.”
He said, “If they have come out for peace, take them alive; and if they have come out for war, take them alive.”
These young officers of the district governors marched out of the city, with the army behind them,
and each one struck down his opponent. The Arameans fled, and Israel pursued them, but Ben-hadad king of Aram escaped on horseback with the cavalry.
Then the king of Israel marched out, attacked the horses and chariots, and inflicted a great defeat on the Arameans.
Afterward the prophet approached the king of Israel and said, “Go, strengthen yourself, and consider carefully what you must do, for in the spring the king of Aram will come up against you.”

Meanwhile the servants of the king of Aram said to him, “Israel’s God is a god of the hills; that is why they were stronger than us. But if we fight them on the plain, surely we will be stronger than they.
So do this: remove each of the kings from his position and replace them with commanders.
And raise an army like the one you lost, horse for horse and chariot for chariot, so we can fight them on the plain; surely we will be stronger than they.” The king listened to them and did so.
In the spring, Ben-hadad mustered the Arameans and went up to Aphek to fight against Israel.
The Israelites were also mustered and provisioned, and went out to meet them. The Israelites camped opposite them like two little flocks of goats, while the Arameans filled the countryside.

Then the man of God came and said to the king of Israel, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Because the Arameans have said the LORD is a god of the hills and not of the valleys, I will deliver all this great army into your hand, and you will know that I am the LORD.’”
For seven days they camped opposite each other, and on the seventh day the battle was joined, and the Israelites struck down 100,000 Aramean foot soldiers in one day.
The rest fled into the city of Aphek, where the wall fell on 27,000 of those who remained. And Ben-hadad fled and hid in an inner room in the city.

His servants said to him, “Look, we have heard that the kings of the house of Israel are merciful. Let us put sackcloth around our waists and ropes around our heads, and go out to the king of Israel; perhaps he will spare your life.”

So they put sackcloth around their waists and ropes around their heads, went to the king of Israel, and said, “Your servant Ben-hadad says, ‘Please let me live.’” And the king answered, “Is he still alive? He is my brother.”

Now the men took this as a good sign and quickly grasped at his word, saying, “Yes, Ben-hadad is your brother.” The king said, “Go and bring him.” So Ben-hadad came out, and the king had him come up into the chariot.

Ben-hadad said to him, “I will restore the cities my father took from your father, and you may set up your own marketplaces in Damascus, as my father did in Samaria.” “On these terms I will release you,” Ahab said. So he made a treaty with him and let him go.
By the word of the LORD, one of the sons of the prophets said to his companion, “Strike me, please.” But the man refused to strike him.
So the prophet said to him, “Because you have not obeyed the voice of the LORD, as soon as you leave me a lion will kill you.” And when the man left, a lion found him and killed him.
Then the prophet found another man and said, “Strike me, please.” So the man struck him and wounded him.
Then the prophet went and stood waiting for the king on the road, disguising himself with a bandage over his eyes.

As the king passed by, he cried out to the king, “Your servant went out into the middle of the battle, and suddenly a man brought me a captive and said, ‘Guard this man; if he is missing, your life will be for his life, or you must pay a talent of silver.’
But while your servant was busy here and there, the man disappeared.” And the king of Israel said to him, “So shall your judgment be; you yourself have decided it.”
Then the prophet quickly removed the bandage from his eyes, and the king of Israel recognized him as one of the prophets.
And he said to the king, “This is what the LORD says: ‘Because you have let slip from your hand the man I had devoted to destruction, your life will be for his life, and your people for his people.’”
Sullen and angry, the king of Israel went home to Samaria.
